text: And we are back on the showroom floor here at reInvent 2019 in Las Vegas and I can tell you it
is just a buzzing environment here and now we're gonna get into some really serious
architecture deep dives and I'm actually joined by Peter McGuire from Ecofit CTO right?

That's right yeah. How are you enjoying the show? This is great this is my first time ever at reInvent so it's a lot of walking and I brought some good shoes I'm happy about that but I'm having an awesome time.
You're already doing really well that's fantastic. Tell us a little bit about what Ecofit does.

Sure Ecofit does automated asset tracking for the fitness industry. We put sensors on all of the equipment in the gyms and we track utilization of that. We do processing at the cloud and generate reports which are valuable for our end customers.
And that's telling them maybe what they want to buy and how they might want to rotate their equipment.
Writing off a big trend here right Peter? You clearly see you know Fitbits everybody wants to know more about their health but also actually solving a problem for the actual clubs.
Yeah. That own the equipment right?

Yeah it's a niche market but it's a good one. Everybody wants data and particularly these gym chains are spending a lot of money on their fitness equipment to elongate those lives to make better purchasing decisions is going to save them a lot of money.
Right. Now I know that you know speaking with you before you did a fair bit of development in this space and you originally used servers.
Yeah.
A very traditional development method.
We did. Yeah.
How did that work out for you?
I mean it got us started up but it really started to fall apart when we started to scale.
So when we had 10, 15, 20 facilities in the very early days.
Okay fine.
We were having problems.
Things started to fail.
And our ability to go into these monolithic applications we had running.
We were on EC2 but we were running these big applications and solving them was really challenging.
So we moved we took the jump we took the plunge and we went into serverless.
Well you know what Peter?
Let's get into it.
Okay.
I can't wait.
Walk us through it.
Sure.
So you can see here where initially our data comes in from the sensors here and runs to a gateway.
So each piece of equipment has a sensor and that runs to a gateway and the gateway is running green grass.
That's how we do our deployments and that's how we do all of our maintenance.
We have lambdas running on the green grass.
Yep.
And that runs through to two different areas.
It runs to IoT Core.
That's for more real-time driving sensor information data that's going to go into our sensor health which I'll get to.
And it also goes through API Gateway to our utilization Lambda which does the crunching of the numbers to make sure that creates the data that we need in our data lake in our database.
So you're using a lot of the IoT services.
Yeah.
Like authentication.
Oh yeah.
All taken care of.
Certificate security management which is fantastic.
Don't need to worry about that.
It's good.
Yeah.
What is this layer doing up here?
Okay.
So this is just the installation layer.
We have an app that we've created that also consumes data through API Gateway from a back end using Lambdas.
And installers will use this to deploy our systems in the gym.
There's another path that happens here.
This is a restful interface that actually goes straight to the Gateway through a third-party server.
And that gives the app real-time information.
So you're configuring those sensors up here.
Yeah.
You're monitoring and getting the utilization.
What if some of those go offline?
That's the big thing.
In every IoT system, particularly in this case.
If a sensor goes offline or if a gateway goes offline, it'll happen.
It's guaranteed.
We need to know about it.
Right.
And so we will have information driving through IoT Core.
It's driving to our sensor health Lambda.
And this is updating to SQS and using that as a queue.
If we don't get the information in a certain amount of time from an entire Gateway, then we know that Gateway is probably down.
We have to act on that very quick.
So we have notifications going out through this is actually SNS that goes straight to us to tell us there's something wrong with the system.
Yeah. And of course, buffering is a big benefit of using Greengrass.
It's critical.
When it comes back online, everything syncs again.
That's correct.
All right. What's going on over here? Because I see some reporting.
Some reporting and this is the big meat of what we deliver to our customers is our reports.
So we crunch all the data.
We store it in a time series database here.
And then we consume it out two different ways.
One is an automated reporting process that we run with Lambda.
You can see it right there.
There's our reporting Lambdas.
And that generates reports and it goes out to our portal.
The other one is connecting actual BI tools, business intelligence tools to this database and consuming all of that data and generating offline reports.
Great stuff. So all your central source of truth in this database.
That's right.
Time series.
You're using serverless everywhere.
I love it, Peter.
Thank you for coming to share your architecture with us.
No worries.
And thank you for joining us on This is My Architecture Live.
Thank you.
